class_schedule.topic: Core Mechanics and the Atom of Gameplay
learning_objectives: Define core mechanics and distinguish them from secondary mechanics
Identify the core mechanic in a variety of published games
Analyze how a single core mechanic generates diverse player experiences
readings: Game Mechanics: Advanced Game Design by Adams & Dormans, Ch. 1-2
A Game Design Vocabulary by Anthropy & Clark, Ch. 3

lecture_notes.introduction: Welcome to Game Theory and Mechanics. Building on the foundations from GD101, this course examines the formal systems that make games work, from the smallest mechanical interaction to large-scale progression arcs.
key_concepts: Core mechanic: the single repeatable action that defines the moment-to-moment gameplay
Secondary mechanics: actions that support or modify the core mechanic
Verbs as mechanics: jump, shoot, match, place, negotiate
The relationship between mechanics and player agency
Atomic game design: composing complex systems from simple mechanical atoms
lecture_notes.demonstration: Side-by-side analysis of core mechanics in Pac-Man (navigate and consume), Tetris (rotate and place), and Chess (move and capture)
setup_requirements: Access to at least two different games for hands-on analysis
Design journal from GD101 or new notebook

review.value: The fundamental, repeatable player action around which the entire game is built; changing the core mechanic changes the game's identity
A mechanic has depth when it is easy to understand but supports a wide range of strategies and outcomes

learning_objectives: Distinguish between positive and negative feedback loops in game systems
Predict the behavioral consequences of each loop type on match pacing
Design a simple system that uses both loop types intentionally
readings: Game Mechanics: Advanced Game Design by Adams & Dormans, Ch. 4
Characteristics of Games by Elias, Garfield & Gutschera, Ch. 7

lecture_notes.introduction: Feedback loops are the invisible engines that drive game pacing. Understanding them is essential for controlling whether a game snowballs toward a decisive conclusion or rubber-bands toward a close finish.
key_concepts: Positive feedback loops: success breeds more success, accelerating toward an endpoint (e.g., Monopoly's property accumulation)
Negative feedback loops: mechanisms that slow leaders and help trailing players, extending competition (e.g., Mario Kart's item distribution)
Runaway leaders and caught-up losers: the emotional cost of unchecked loops
Combining loops: using positive loops for short-term excitement and negative loops for long-term balance
Hidden feedback: systems where the loop is not immediately apparent to the player
lecture_notes.demonstration: Simulating a simple economy game with and without a negative feedback mechanism, tracking point divergence over 10 rounds
setup_requirements: Tokens and score sheets for in-class simulation

review.value: A system where gaining an advantage makes it easier to gain further advantage, accelerating outcomes (destabilizing)
A system where gaining an advantage triggers counterforces that reduce that advantage, promoting equilibrium (stabilizing)

class_schedule.topic: Game Balance: Symmetry, Asymmetry, and Fairness
learning_objectives: Differentiate between symmetric and asymmetric game balance
Apply transitive and intransitive relationship models to balance design
Identify common balance problems in multiplayer games
readings: Game Mechanics: Advanced Game Design by Adams & Dormans, Ch. 6
Game Balance by Schreiber & Brathwaite, Ch. 1-3

lecture_notes.introduction: Balance is the art of making a game fair without making it boring. Today we examine the mathematical and perceptual tools designers use to achieve balance.
key_concepts: Symmetric balance: all players start with identical resources and options (Chess, Go)
Asymmetric balance: players have different abilities but equivalent chances of winning (StarCraft factions)
Transitive relationships: strict power hierarchies (Rock beats Scissors)
Intransitive relationships: circular dominance patterns (Rock-Paper-Scissors) that prevent dominant strategies
Fruity versus crunchy balance: perceived fairness matters as much as mathematical fairness
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyzing StarCraft II's Terran/Protoss/Zerg asymmetric balance using win-rate data and unit cost curves
setup_requirements: Calculator or spreadsheet for cost-efficiency comparisons

review.value: Giving players different abilities, resources, or rules while ensuring each option has a roughly equal probability of winning through complementary strengths and weaknesses
Circular dominance (A beats B, B beats C, C beats A) that prevents any single option from being universally dominant

learning_objectives: Design reward schedules using principles from behavioral psychology
Calculate expected value and variance for probability-based reward systems
Evaluate the ethical implications of variable-ratio reward schedules
readings: Game Mechanics: Advanced Game Design by Adams & Dormans, Ch. 8
Actionable Gamification by Yu-kai Chou, Ch. 4-5

lecture_notes.introduction: Rewards are a game's way of communicating value and progress. How and when you deliver rewards profoundly shapes player behavior and retention.
key_concepts: Fixed-ratio schedules: reward every N actions (e.g., XP per kill)
Variable-ratio schedules: reward on unpredictable intervals (e.g., loot drops, gacha)
Expected value: probability times magnitude, the average payoff of a random event
Variance and risk: high-variance rewards feel more exciting but can frustrate
Ethical design: the line between engagement and exploitation in variable-ratio systems
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a loot-drop probability table with expected value calculations, then simulating 100 drops to compare theoretical and actual distributions
setup_requirements: Spreadsheet or calculator for probability modeling
Dice sets (d6, d20) for physical simulation

review.value: Rewards delivered after an unpredictable number of actions; produces the highest response rate and strongest habit formation but carries ethical risk
The long-run average outcome of a random event, calculated as the sum of (probability of each outcome multiplied by its value)

learning_objectives: Design a progression system that maintains player engagement over time
Model difficulty curves and identify common curve shapes
Apply the concept of gating to control player access to content
readings: Game Balance by Schreiber & Brathwaite, Ch. 8-9
The Art of Game Design by Jesse Schell, Ch. 15

lecture_notes.introduction: Progression is the long arc of a player's journey through a game. A well-designed progression system keeps the player engaged from the first minute to the last hour.
key_concepts: Linear versus open progression: corridors versus sandboxes
Experience curves: linear, exponential, and S-curve XP requirements
Difficulty curves: sawtooth (rising tension with relief valleys), staircase (plateaus with jumps), and smooth ramp
Gating: skill gates, resource gates, narrative gates, and time gates
Power creep: the long-term balance threat in games with progression
lecture_notes.demonstration: Graphing and comparing XP curves from Diablo II (exponential), Dark Souls (staircase difficulty), and Celeste (sawtooth with assists)

review.value: A graph of challenge intensity over time; the shape determines pacing, and mismatched curves cause players to quit from boredom (too flat) or frustration (too steep)
The gradual increase in the power level of new content or items, which can invalidate older content and destabilize balance if not managed

learning_objectives: Define emergent gameplay and distinguish it from scripted content
Identify the system properties that enable emergence
Design a small rule set that produces emergent behavior
readings: Rules of Play by Salen & Zimmerman, Ch. 22
Emergent Gameplay in Game Design (Joris Dormans, GDC Talk)

lecture_notes.introduction: The most memorable moments in games are often ones the designer never explicitly created. Emergence is what happens when simple rules interact to produce complex, unpredictable outcomes.
key_concepts: Emergence: complex behavior arising from the interaction of simple rules
Designed emergence versus accidental emergence
Orthogonal mechanics: when independent systems create unexpected combinations (Breath of the Wild's physics-chemistry interactions)
The narrative potential of emergence: player-generated stories (Dwarf Fortress, RimWorld)
The control problem: balancing emergence with designer intent
lecture_notes.demonstration: Conway's Game of Life as a pure emergence demonstration, followed by analysis of how Breath of the Wild's fire, wind, and metal systems create emergent puzzle solutions

review.value: Complex, unscripted behavior and outcomes that arise from the interaction of a game's simple, well-defined systems rather than from explicit designer authorship
Independent game systems that interact in meaningful ways, multiplying the possibility space beyond what any single system provides

quizzes.topic: Core Mechanics, Feedback Loops, and Game Balance

questions.question: Monopoly is often criticized for having a strong positive feedback loop. Identify the specific mechanic that creates this loop and propose one rule modification that would introduce a negative feedback loop to counterbalance it.
questions.answer: The mechanic is property ownership combined with rent: owning more properties generates more rent income, which allows buying more properties, which generates even more rent. This is a classic positive feedback loop because advantage compounds. A negative feedback loop modification would be a progressive tax: players who own more than a threshold number of properties pay a maintenance fee proportional to their holdings at the start of each turn. This drains resources from the leader and gives trailing players more time to acquire properties, slowing the runaway-leader problem.

rubric.key: Correctly identifies the property-rent positive feedback mechanic
Proposed rule creates a genuine negative feedback loop
Explains how the modification counterbalances the original loop

questions.question: Explain the difference between transitive and intransitive balance relationships and give one example of each from a published game.
questions.answer: Transitive balance creates a strict hierarchy: option A is always better than option B, which is always better than option C. Example: in many RPGs, a Steel Sword has strictly higher damage than an Iron Sword, creating a transitive upgrade path. Intransitive balance creates a circular dominance: A beats B, B beats C, but C beats A, so no single option dominates. Example: in Pokemon, Water beats Fire, Fire beats Grass, Grass beats Water, ensuring no single type is universally strongest.

rubric.key: Correct definitions of both transitive and intransitive relationships
Accurate transitive example from a real game
Accurate intransitive example from a real game

quizzes.topic: Reward Systems, Progression, and Difficulty Design

questions.question: A mobile game uses a gacha system where players spend premium currency for a 2% chance of a top-tier character, 18% chance of a mid-tier character, and 80% chance of a low-tier character. If top-tier is worth 100 value units, mid-tier is worth 20, and low-tier is worth 1, calculate the expected value of a single pull. Then explain why players continue pulling despite a low expected value.
questions.answer: Expected value = (0.02 x 100) + (0.18 x 20) + (0.80 x 1) = 2 + 3.6 + 0.8 = 6.4 value units per pull. Players continue despite the low average because variable-ratio reinforcement creates strong habit formation. The unpredictable timing of a rare, high-value reward produces intermittent reinforcement, which behavioral psychology has shown generates the most persistent response patterns. Additionally, near-miss effects (getting a mid-tier when hoping for top-tier) sustain hope and perceived closeness to a big win.

rubric.key: References variable-ratio reinforcement or intermittent reinforcement

questions.question: Describe the 'sawtooth' difficulty curve pattern and explain why it is effective for maintaining player engagement over long play sessions.
questions.answer: A sawtooth difficulty curve alternates between periods of rising challenge (the incline of each tooth) and brief drops in intensity (the valley between teeth). Each peak is slightly higher than the previous one, so overall difficulty trends upward. This pattern is effective because the rising sections push players toward the edge of their skill (creating flow and accomplishment), while the valleys provide relief that prevents fatigue and frustration. The valleys also serve as consolidation periods where players can practice new skills in a lower-pressure context before the next escalation. This rhythm mirrors natural attention cycles and prevents the monotony of a smooth, continuous ramp.

rubric.key: Accurately describes the sawtooth shape with peaks and valleys
Explains the tension-relief cycle and its effect on engagement
Connects pattern to flow, fatigue prevention, or skill consolidation

major_assignments.title: Mechanical Systems Analysis and Redesign
major_assignments.description: Select a published game with a known balance or pacing problem. Perform a formal systems analysis identifying the feedback loops, balance relationships, and reward structures. Then propose and model a mechanical redesign that addresses the identified problems while preserving the game's core identity.
objectives: Apply formal systems analysis tools (feedback loop diagrams, balance matrices) to a real game
Identify root causes of balance or pacing problems at the mechanical level
Design and model a mechanical solution using quantitative reasoning
Communicate complex system interactions clearly in diagrams and writing
deliverables: Systems diagram of the original game's core mechanics, feedback loops, and reward structures
Written analysis (4-6 pages) identifying specific balance or pacing problems with evidence
Proposed redesign with annotated systems diagram showing changes
Spreadsheet model demonstrating the quantitative impact of the redesign over a simulated 20-round session

criteria: Accurate identification of core and secondary mechanics
Complete feedback loop diagram with correctly labeled positive and negative loops
Balance relationships mapped with transitive and intransitive labels
Clear identification of the problem with supporting evidence
Proposed changes target root causes rather than symptoms
Redesign preserves the game's core identity and appeal
Changes are specific and implementable, not vague suggestions
Potential side effects of changes are acknowledged and addressed
Spreadsheet model correctly simulates game rounds
Before and after comparison clearly shows impact of changes
Assumptions are stated and reasonable
Variance and edge cases are considered
Diagrams are clear, well-labeled, and use consistent notation
Written analysis is logically structured and persuasive
Technical terminology is used correctly throughout
Professional formatting and presentation
Thorough and accurate systems analysis of the chosen game
Insightful identification of root mechanical problems
Well-reasoned redesign supported by quantitative modeling
Clear, professional communication in both diagrams and writing

major_assignments.title: Emergent Mechanics Tabletop Game
major_assignments.description: Design and build a tabletop game with no more than 5 rules that produces emergent gameplay. The game must be playable in 15-20 minutes and must demonstrate that simple, orthogonal mechanics can generate complex and surprising outcomes. Conduct playtests and document emergent behaviors that arose during play.
objectives: Design a minimal rule set that produces emergent complexity
Demonstrate understanding of orthogonal mechanics and system interactions
Observe and document emergent behaviors in playtest sessions
Evaluate the trade-off between designer control and emergent freedom
deliverables: Complete playable tabletop game with all physical components
Rules document (one page maximum) with no more than 5 rules
Playtest log documenting at least 5 play sessions with notes on emergent behaviors observed
Reflection essay (3-4 pages) analyzing which system interactions produced emergence and what surprised you

criteria: Five or fewer rules that are clear and unambiguous
Rules create orthogonal interactions that enable emergence
Game is playable and completable in 15-20 minutes
Design demonstrates creativity and originality
Documented emergent behaviors are genuine and non-trivial
Emergence arises from system interactions, not randomness alone
Multiple distinct emergent strategies or outcomes observed
Game produces different experiences across play sessions
At least 5 sessions documented with specific observations
Notes distinguish between designed behavior and emergent behavior
Quantitative data collected where relevant
Iterative changes noted if any were made
Thoughtful analysis of which interactions produced emergence
Honest assessment of what worked and what fell flat
Connections drawn to course concepts and readings
Professional writing quality
Elegant rule set that produces genuine emergent complexity
Well-documented playtesting with rich observational notes
Insightful reflection connecting practice to theory
Polished, playable game that is enjoyable to experience

weekly_quizzes.description: Regular assessment of theoretical knowledge covering mechanics, feedback loops, balance, and probability

systems_analysis_assignment.description: Formal analysis and redesign of a published game's mechanical systems with quantitative modeling

emergent_mechanics_game.description: Design, build, playtest, and document an original tabletop game that produces emergent gameplay from minimal rules

class_participation.description: Active engagement in discussions, in-class exercises, and peer feedback sessions

final_examination.description: Comprehensive exam covering all course topics including mechanical analysis, balance modeling, and systems design

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is expected for all sessions. In-class game analysis exercises and peer playtests cannot be replicated outside of class. More than 3 unexcused absences will reduce the final grade by one half-letter.
course_policies.late_submissions: Assignments are penalized 10% per day late. The emergent mechanics game must be submitted on time as in-class presentation slots cannot be rescheduled.
course_policies.collaboration: Discussion of concepts is encouraged. Written analyses and the final exam must be individual work. Tabletop game design may be done in pairs if approved by the instructor.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All written work must be original. External sources, including published game analyses, must be cited. Fabricating playtest data or presenting another designer's mechanics as your own will result in a zero and academic referral.
course_policies.materials: Students should have access to a spreadsheet application for quantitative modeling. Basic prototyping supplies (cards, dice, tokens) are needed for the tabletop game project. No programming is required.
